## Full Transcript (cleaned from auto-captions)

**[Narrator]** Most good spy stories have a secret message, usually in code. But a master spy can always crack it. Today, code breaking involves complex mathematics and relies on powerful algorithms. But even with all that power, there's one coded message no one has been able to crack. Ten feet tall and sitting in plain view in the backyard of the CIA. Correspondent Chad Cohen is on the case.

**[Chad Cohen]** Of all the secrets hidden at the highly secured CIA headquarters in Langley, Virginia, you may be looking at one of the strangest. Meet Kryptos. It's a copper monolith inscribed with nearly 2,000 very random-looking characters. But there's a message here, if you can break the code. And despite countless attempts since Kryptos's 1989 installation, that has yet to be done.

Now, you're the Central Intelligence Agency, right? You're supposed to be very intelligent. Everybody there is intelligent, right? You don't have anything pulled over your eyes?

Well, that's at least what we all like to believe. But with Kryptos, that's exactly what Jim Sanborn has done. And he is not the world's most sophisticated cryptographer. In fact, Sanborn is not a cryptographer at all. The mastermind behind Kryptos is an artist — if an unconventional one. His workspace looks more science lab than art studio.

**[Jim Sanborn]** I work with lodestones, compasses, and the Earth's magnetic field, the Coriolis force. These were natural forces that were invisible. Those kinds of things fascinated me, and I set about to try to make those things visible for people.

**[Cohen]** When the CIA was looking for a sculpture to grace the courtyard of their new headquarters building, Sanborn wanted to design something that would stay invisible — a secret code. So how does an artist craft a message that the best code breakers in the world can't crack?

**[Ed Scheidt]** I set the codes up for him, which means he didn't have to know the mathematics of it.

**[Cohen]** Sanborn found help in the former chairman of the CIA Cryptographic Center, Ed Scheidt, who gave Jim a crash course in Cryptography 101.

**[Scheidt]** Classical cryptography, and it's been defined this way, includes substitution and includes transposition.

**[Cohen]** For substitution, take one letter or word and swap it for another. Transposition: keep the letters the same, just systematically mix them up. Pretty straightforward tricks, and yet they've been stumping would-be code breakers for thousands of years.

Julius Caesar came up with a system based on substitution to send secret messages to his generals on the battlefield. It's called the Caesar cipher. A letter is substituted with another letter by laying two alphabets on top of each other and shifting one of them over a set number of places. You get a whole new alphabet. The word "film" in this case now becomes "ilop" — complete gobbledygook if intercepted by an enemy. But if you know how many spaces to shift the alphabet over, it's clear as day.

The codes have gotten more complicated through the ages. Nazi Germany's Enigma machine, for example, which Hitler used successfully for years before Allied code breakers cracked it, employed a mechanical rotor system. But ultimately it just created elaborate substitutions.

Transposition ciphers have their place in history too. To send secret messages to his army, General Ulysses S. Grant didn't substitute letters — he just carefully mixed them all up.

Sanborn also employed this technique in Kryptos. Using pen and paper.

**[Sanborn]** So this is the plaintext. This is the way it's written out in English.

**[Cohen]** By writing the message on a grid and transposing it to different positions on a new grid, it becomes unreadable to someone trying to intercept it. And the more times you do it, the more complex it can get.

**[Sanborn]** So it can get very complicated very quickly.

**[Cohen]** Very quickly complicated — maybe to a code breaker. But it's very simple to the intended recipient. If the code is meant for you, you don't have to break it. Because when two parties want to communicate via cipher, they agree ahead of time on the specific rules of the system. With transposition, you might agree on how many rows you'd use, or how many times the chart is flipped. With substitution, you might agree on how many spaces to offset the alphabet.

To make Kryptos, Sanborn took both these techniques, used them to encode four distinct messages, and cut them into copper. Every letter was cut out with a jigsaw and took two and a half years of cutting to do it.

Secure as the CIA may be, it wasn't long before Kryptos went mainstream. Through internet message boards, websites, hacker conventions — Kryptos has become a phenomenon.

**[Elonka Dunin]** We have a group of about a thousand people around the world that are brainstorming this very, very heavily.

**[Cohen]** Elonka Dunin, whose website has had millions of hits from would-be crackers, is generally considered to be the leading Kryptos expert in the world — even Sanborn says so. But she's only seen the sculpture up close once.

**[Dunin]** Well, because we can't get in to see Kryptos at CIA, because it's at the center of CIA.

**[Cohen]** So Elonka took me here to the Hirshhorn Museum in Washington DC, to see a similar Sanborn piece called Antipodes.

**[Dunin]** So this is the next best thing. The order of the letters is identical. Part one starts here where it says E-M-U-F-P. [That] decrypts to the letters B-E-T-W-E-E-N. "Between."

**[Cohen]** Since E-M-U-F-P doesn't resemble anything like the word "between" — well, since Parts 1, 2, and 3 of Kryptos were cracked in 1999, it all looks like pretty much garbage to the untrained eye. And here's the guy who did it. LA computer scientist Jim Gillogly.

**[Gillogly]** Part of the reason that it's developed a cult following is that I was able to crack it and expose it to the world. So people are able to say: "Look, some of it really is solvable. It's not just a mass of garbage letters."

**[Cohen]** Gillogly's programs combine algorithms with rules of thumb for English grammar and spelling. The computer tries millions of different character combinations and throws away the ones that look like nonsense.

He's cracked many codes in his life, but he always starts the same way — with what's called a frequency count. Simply put, he counts up all the letters in the text and sees how often each one occurs. That works because in any language, certain letters always show up more often than others — whether it's a cookbook, a flight manual, or a page from Hamlet. The frequency of each letter will always stay about the same. So in English, on average, of all the letters in any given bit of text, E's make up over 12 percent, while Q's occur much less frequently — less than 0.2 percent.

When Gillogly studied the first two parts of Kryptos, he noticed there were just as many Q's as there were E's. It was a tip-off that they were probably standing in for — or substituting for — more common letters.

Gillogly ran his program on it. "Okay, so you typed this stuff in, you typed in that code, and boom, this came out?" "This came out."

But as the solution for Parts 1 and 2 came out on Jim Gillogly's screen, the mystery just got deeper. "Between subtle shading and the absence of light lies the nuance of illusion." "It was totally invisible. How's that possible? They used the Earth's magnetic field. The information was gathered and transmitted to an unknown location." "This was his last message."

It's another one to scratch your head. Yes, there's a lot of "hmms" here. There was nothing to help reveal the meaning of the sculpture — just the ambiguous words of Jim Sanborn. Not that it mattered to the man who actually cracked it.

**[Gillogly]** For myself, personally, I don't care quite so much about what was being hidden, as to how it was hidden.

**[Cohen]** Gillogly quickly discovered that Part 3 of Kryptos was hidden in a radically different way. This one had lots of E's and only one Q — just like it would be in English.

**[Gillogly]** I could tell that it was a transposition cipher because the frequency of the letters was identical to the frequency in English.

**[Cohen]** Remember what Sanborn was doing in his studio — the transposition he did with that graph paper? Gillogly created a computer program that undid it. And eventually he cracked Part 3 of Kryptos as well.

This section was recognizable — taken right out of archaeologist Howard Carter's notebook, describing the moment he first opened King Tut's tomb: "Slowly, slowly, desperately slowly, the remains of passage debris that encumbered the lower part of the doorway was removed. And then, widening the hole a little, I inserted the candle and peered in. The hot air escaping from the chamber caused the flame to flicker, but presently details of the room within emerged from the mist."

Diary excerpts from famous explorers, mysterious allusions to light and dark — even when Sanborn's words are put in the right order, they are cryptic, to say the least. "Between subtle shadings" and the Howard Carter — what does it mean?

**[Dunin]** Well, they all tend to have a common theme of things hidden, things underground, things concealed from sight. So that theme may be something that is going to give us a clue towards Part 4.

**[Cohen]** Part 4 is made up of the final 98 letters on the sculpture. And only by breaking that last piece will the true message of Kryptos be revealed. But so far it has proven impervious to all the techniques of would-be code breakers.

Part 4 is hard. Well, number one, because it's very short. It's only 97 or 98 characters.

**[Gillogly]** When cryptanalysts are working on a code, we generally have large amounts of ciphertext to work with. Then you're able to look for the very subtle mathematical patterns which is what takes a code apart.

**[Cohen]** Is it something really simple that people are missing?

**[Sanborn]** No. Maybe. Not maybe. Maybe not.

**[Cohen]** And he may have had one more trick up his sleeve — something called concealment.

**[Dunin]** It could be a matter of obfuscating the language before starting to encrypt. Ed Scheidt has said that he used a little bit of concealment on Part 4, but we don't know what that means.

**[Cohen]** It can be concealed in various ways before enciphering it, to make it even harder to break. The first challenge, then, is: well, what masking technique was used? Techniques like removing all the vowels first, or spelling the message phonetically, can radically undermine the code-breaking methods.

So it may still be a long time before anyone experiences the thrill of cracking all of Kryptos. Even Ed, who taught Jim how to make code, doesn't know the solution — which makes Jim Sanborn the only person on Earth who does. And he isn't dropping any hints.

**[Sanborn]** It wouldn't help to just kind of get it out. I burned myself.

**[Cohen]** I mean, I really like your program, but I'm sorry.

[Music / Applause]

## Analysis: Key Statements and Implications for K4

### 1. Sanborn on the transposition process (ON CAMERA, showing his working charts)

> "So this is the plaintext. This is the way it's written out in English."

He is physically shown writing plaintext on a grid and transposing to a new grid. This is **visual confirmation of the grid transposition method** for K3. The narrator notes "the more times you do it, the more complex it can get," and Sanborn agrees: "It can get very complicated very quickly."

**Implication**: Sanborn's working method involves GRID-BASED transposition — writing on one grid, reading off another. This is consistent with the Cardan grille hypothesis (a grid with holes defining reading order). The "charts of K3" mentioned in the video description may show his actual working grids.

### 2. Scheidt on his role

> "I set the codes up for him, which means he didn't have to know the mathematics of it."

This is Scheidt ON CAMERA saying he designed the system so Sanborn could execute it without understanding the math. Consistent with all other sources: Scheidt = method designer, Sanborn = parameter chooser and physical executor.

### 3. Gillogly on K3 detection

> "I could tell that it was a transposition cipher because the frequency of the letters was identical to the frequency in English."

This is the key diagnostic: K3's letter frequencies match English because transposition preserves frequencies. **K4's frequencies do NOT match English** — which means K4 has a substitution layer (frequency-destroying) in addition to or instead of pure transposition.

### 4. Elonka on concealment

> "Ed Scheidt has said that he used a little bit of concealment on Part 4, but we don't know what that means."

"A little bit of concealment" — this is Scheidt's own characterization, relayed by Dunin. Not heavy concealment, not radical obfuscation. Just "a little bit." This aligns with the model: substitution (Vigenere) + transposition (scramble), where the transposition is the "concealment" that destroys the frequency signature the substitution would otherwise leave.

### 5. Cohen on K4 length

> "Part 4 is made up of the final 98 letters on the sculpture."

Then immediately:

> "It's only 97 or 98 characters."

The narrator says 98, then hedges to 97-or-98. This reflects the question mark ambiguity (97 alphabetic + 1 question mark = 98 total characters). Consistent with Sanborn's "try both."

### 6. Sanborn's evasion

> **Cohen**: "Is it something really simple that people are missing?"
> **Sanborn**: "No. Maybe. Not maybe. Maybe not."

Classic Sanborn non-answer. But note: he doesn't flatly say "no" — he oscillates. The "maybe" suggests there IS a simple insight being missed, even if the full solution isn't simple.

### 7. Sanborn on revealing the answer

> "It wouldn't help to just kind of get it out. I burned myself."

"I burned myself" — possibly referring to the 2010 BERLINCLOCK clue release, or more generally to past hints that he felt gave away too much. He regrets releasing information.

### 8. Dunin on Antipodes at Hirshhorn

> "The order of the letters is identical."

Confirms Antipodes = identical letter sequence to Kryptos cipher side. Our reconstruction (`memory/antipodes_reconstruction.md`) verified this: 1,584 letters, ZERO mismatches.

### 9. Narrator on the "two techniques"

> "Sanborn took both these techniques [substitution and transposition], used them to encode four distinct messages."

This is the narrator's summary, not a direct Sanborn quote. But it confirms the public framing: K1-K4 each use some combination of substitution and transposition. K1/K2 = Vigenere (substitution), K3 = transposition, K4 = ??? (both?).

---

## Relevance to Current Paradigm (Model 2: PT → Cipher → Scramble → Carved Text)

This NOVA segment provides visual evidence that is **strongly consistent** with our scrambling model:

1. **Sanborn is SHOWN doing grid transposition with graph paper** — writing plaintext on one grid, producing ciphertext on another. This is exactly the physical process of applying a Cardan grille or grid-based reading order.

2. **"The more times you do it, the more complex it can get"** — suggests Sanborn may apply transposition MORE THAN ONCE, or combine it with substitution. Our model has substitution THEN transposition (scramble).

3. **"A little bit of concealment"** from Scheidt — the scramble step IS the concealment. It destroys the frequency patterns that would otherwise reveal the Vigenere key.

4. **Sanborn's "maybe/maybe not" on simplicity** — the individual components (Vigenere + grid transposition) ARE simple. The combination, especially with an unknown reading order, is what makes it hard.

---

## Date and Context

This appears to be from NOVA scienceNow Season 2, which aired in **2005-2006** on PBS. The segment was filmed before Sanborn released any K4 clues (BERLINCLOCK came in 2010, EASTNORTHEAST in 2014). At this point, K4 was completely uncracked with no known cribs.
